agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving most critical business parameters undefined.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing / Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time demand-based delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined metric representing current delivery request volume relative to some baseline — exact measurement method unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds unspecified" },
      { "term": "Real-time", "definition": "Prices adjust dynamically as demand changes, not on a fixed schedule — latency tolerance unspecified" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No manual human intervention required for price adjustments under normal operation" },
      { "term": "Competitive", "definition": "Prices should not significantly exceed market rates, implying awareness of competitor pricing or customer price sensitivity" },
      { "term": "Profitable", "definition": "Prices must remain above the cost of fulfillment to ensure positive margin on each delivery" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Prices must adjust in response to changes in demand — higher demand produces higher prices, lower demand produces lower prices",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core business policy of the product. The direction of the correlation (demand up → price up) is a policy choice, not a physical constraint. A VP could decide to invert this for a promotional period or hold prices flat.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D1 > D2 for the same route/time, the computed price at D1 must be >= the computed price at D2"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real-time — prices reflect current demand, not stale or batch-computed demand",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time is a latency policy. The business could decide daily recalculation is sufficient. It does not corrupt system state if delayed.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand changes, the price offered to the next customer must reflect the updated demand within the system's defined latency window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The pricing system must operate without manual human intervention under normal conditions",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is an operational policy. A human-in-the-loop fallback does not corrupt state — it's a design preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are computed and applied by the system without requiring human approval for each change"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A price floor must exist — delivery price must never fall below the cost of fulfillment (ensuring profitability per delivery)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Selling below cost without explicit authorization corrupts the financial integrity of the business. A delivery priced below its fulfillment cost is a guaranteed loss and violates the stated goal of profitability. This is a hard financial constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any delivery, computed_price >= fulfillment_cost for that delivery"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A price ceiling must exist — delivery price must never exceed a maximum guardrail threshold",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "An unbounded price is a system integrity failure. If the algorithm produces a price of $10,000 for a local delivery, the system is in a corrupt state. The specific ceiling value is policy (not invariant), but the existence of a ceiling is an invariant — the system must enforce some upper bound.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any delivery, computed_price <= configured_maximum_price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative price is physically nonsensical for a delivery service. It represents corrupt state — the system should never offer to pay a customer to receive a delivery.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any delivery, computed_price > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The demand signal used for pricing must be based on actual, current market conditions — not fabricated, stale, or manipulable data",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the demand signal is corrupted or fabricated, every price derived from it is invalid. Data integrity of the demand input is a hard constraint on system correctness.",
      "testable_assertion": "The demand metric used for price computation must be derived from verifiable order/request data within the defined recency window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Once a price is quoted and accepted by a customer, it must be honored for that transaction regardless of subsequent demand changes",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Changing a confirmed price after customer acceptance is a contractual and trust violation. The quoted-and-accepted price is an immutable commitment. This is a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "A delivery's price at the time of customer confirmation must equal the price charged at fulfillment"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., concert ends, natural disaster)", "expected_behavior": "Price ceiling guardrail caps the surge; system does not produce exploitative pricing during emergencies" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price floor guardrail ensures price never drops below fulfillment cost" },
    { "scenario": "Demand signal becomes unavailable or stale (system outage)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — should the system fall back to a default/base price, or halt quoting entirely? CEO description does not address this." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request the same route at the same instant with demand changing between them", "expected_behavior": "Race condition — both should receive a consistent, valid price. The system must be deterministic for concurrent requests." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging laws triggered during emergency demand", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — no mention of legal/regulatory compliance. Some jurisdictions prohibit surge pricing during declared emergencies." },
    { "scenario": "Competitor drops prices significantly below our floor", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — 'competitive' goal conflicts with 'profitable' goal when competitor prices are below our cost of fulfillment" },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price, clicks to confirm, and demand has changed during that window", "expected_behavior": "The quoted price must be honored for a reasonable hold period — the definition of 'reasonable' is unspecified" },
    { "scenario": "Algorithmic feedback loop — high prices suppress demand, low demand triggers low prices, low prices spike demand, repeat", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — oscillation damping is not addressed. System could enter unstable price cycling." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand and improve utilization during low demand. However, poorly calibrated guardrails risk either leaving money on the table (ceiling too low) or destroying customer trust (ceiling too high). Price floor violation is an existential financial risk.",
    "operational": "Fully automated system reduces manual pricing effort but introduces operational complexity: monitoring, alerting, demand signal reliability, and fallback procedures become critical. Real-time requirement demands low-latency infrastructure.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can cause confusion, frustration, and perception of unfairness. Transparent communication of why prices vary is essential for adoption. Price volatility erodes trust if not managed carefully.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Regulatory risk (price gouging laws), reputational risk (perceived exploitation during emergencies), algorithmic risk (feedback loops, flash crashes), and competitive risk (customers flee to fixed-price competitors). The vagueness of the spec amplifies all risks.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Pay variable prices — may feel exploited during high demand, or get good deals during low demand. Trust and transparency are critical." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Higher demand pricing may increase their compensation during surges (if revenue-sharing exists), or create expectations about surge pay." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Revenue becomes less predictable, more volatile. Forecasting becomes harder. Margin analysis must account for dynamic pricing." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variation. Need clear policies on when/whether to adjust prices retroactively." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must ensure dynamic pricing complies with price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and contractual obligations." },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model to customers transparently. Brand perception at risk if perceived as exploitative." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a 4-sentence vision statement, not a specification. Nearly every critical parameter is undefined: how demand is measured, what the guardrail thresholds are, what 'real-time' latency means, how 'competitive' is measured, what happens during system failures, what the pricing algorithm actually is, and whether regulatory constraints exist. I extracted 8 rules but 5 of them are inferred from business common sense rather than explicitly stated. The only firmly stated facts are: (1) prices go up with demand, (2) prices go down without demand, (3) it's automated, and (4) there will be 'some guardrails.' Everything else is my domain inference.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "BR-1: Demand-price positive correlation is explicitly stated",
      "BR-3: Full automation is explicitly stated",
      "BR-5/BR-6: Some form of guardrails is explicitly stated"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "BR-4: Price floor at cost-of-fulfillment is inferred from 'profitable' — the CEO may mean portfolio-level profitability, not per-delivery",
      "BR-7: Demand signal integrity is domain common sense, not stated",
      "BR-8: Price-locking on acceptance is contractual common sense, not stated",
      "All guardrail thresholds — no values or even ranges provided",
      "Demand measurement methodology — completely undefined",
      "Regulatory compliance — not mentioned at all",
      "Failure/fallback behavior — not addressed",
      "Quote validity window — not addressed"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Profitability means per-delivery positive margin, not just portfolio-level",
      "Guardrails include both a ceiling and a floor",
      "A quoted price must be honored once accepted by a customer",
      "Demand is measured by delivery request volume, not some other proxy",
      "Real-time means sub-minute latency, not daily batch",
      "The system operates within a single regulatory jurisdiction or has jurisdiction-aware rules"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO says 'guardrails so prices don't go crazy' but provides no definition of what constitutes 'crazy.' Without specific floor and ceiling values (or at minimum, a formula), we cannot validate any pricing output as correct or incorrect.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": [
        "Define guardrails as fixed dollar amounts (e.g., $2 floor, $50 ceiling)",
        "Define guardrails as percentage multipliers of a base price (e.g., 0.5x to 3x)",
        "Define guardrails as margin-based (e.g., minimum 15% margin, maximum 300% margin)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Request that Finance and Product jointly define guardrail thresholds as percentage multipliers of a base price per delivery type, reviewed quarterly"
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "How is 'demand' measured? Order volume per hour? Per zone? Per route? Relative to courier supply? Without a clear demand metric, the pricing algorithm has no defined input.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product/Marketing", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Couriers/Drivers"],
      "options": [
        "Demand = order volume per zone per time window",
        "Demand = ratio of orders to available couriers (supply-demand ratio)",
        "Demand = predictive model incorporating historical patterns, weather, events"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Define demand as supply-demand ratio (orders per available courier per zone per 15-minute window) — this most directly reflects the operational constraint"
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory or legal constraints. Many jurisdictions have price gouging laws that prohibit surge pricing during declared emergencies. Dynamic pricing in delivery services has drawn regulatory scrutiny globally.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "Engage legal review before launch to map jurisdiction-specific constraints",
        "Implement emergency override that caps prices at base rate during declared emergencies",
        "Both"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Legal review is mandatory before any implementation begins. Emergency price cap should be a hard requirement."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "What does 'competitive' mean operationally? Are we tracking competitor prices? Is there a maximum premium over market rate? 'Competitive but also profitable' creates a tension that is not resolved.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product/Marketing", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "Ignore competitor pricing — let guardrails handle it",
        "Ingest competitor pricing as an input signal to the algorithm",
        "Set ceiling as a percentage above average market rate"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Clarify with CEO whether 'competitive' means actively tracking competitor prices or simply 'not outrageous.' These are very different system requirements."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback behavior defined. If the demand signal is unavailable, the pricing algorithm fails, or the system is degraded — what price do we charge? Fully automated systems need failure modes.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Couriers/Drivers"],
      "options": [
        "Fall back to a static base price",
        "Fall back to last known good dynamic price",
        "Halt quoting entirely until system recovers"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to static base price — it's the safest option that maintains service availability without risk of corrupt pricing"
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
